content.text: Using the Tech Radar to Navigate the GenAI Ecosystem

The GenAI tooling landscape evolves faster than most enterprise procurement cycles. New foundation models, vector databases, orchestration frameworks, and observability platforms appear weekly. Without a structured evaluation method, teams oscillate between chasing hype and freezing on familiar but outdated choices.

The Technology Radar, popularised by ThoughtWorks, organises technologies into four concentric rings based on maturity and enterprise readiness: Adopt, Trial, Assess, and Hold. Applying this lens to GenAI gives architects a shared vocabulary, forces periodic re-evaluation, and produces a living artefact that communicates risk posture to leadership without lengthy prose.

attrs.alt: GenAI Tech Radar — four-ring diagram with representative tools in each ring

content.text: Ring Semantics
Each ring carries a recommendation that affects procurement, onboarding investment, and the speed at which a team can bet production traffic on the technology.
Ring
Signal
Action
Risk accepted
Adopt
Proven in production at scale
Use as default choice
Minimal — track deprecations
Trial
Positive evidence; gaps still close
Run a bounded pilot with real workload
Medium — may pivot post-pilot
Assess
Promising signals; limited production data
Spike or lab evaluation only
High — do not commit production traffic
Hold
Known problems, superseded, or niche
Freeze new use; migrate existing
N/A — actively reduce exposure
Adopt: Production-Ready GenAI Infrastructure

Technologies in Adopt have survived the enterprise gauntlet: SLA documentation, SOC 2 or equivalent audits, stable versioned APIs, and regression-tested release cycles. Teams should treat them as default choices and focus energy on integration patterns rather than tool evaluation.
Foundation Model APIs
OpenAI GPT-4o / GPT-4o-mini — mature function-calling, vision, structured output with JSON Schema; broad ecosystem support.
Anthropic Claude 3.x (Haiku / Sonnet / Opus) — 200k-token context, strong instruction-following, constitutional safety layer.
Google Gemini 1.5 Pro — 1M-token context; native multimodal (text, image, audio, video, code).
Azure OpenAI Service — enterprise-grade compliance (FedRAMP, HIPAA), private endpoints, VNET integration, same models as OpenAI.
Amazon Bedrock — multi-model access with IAM controls and VPC PrivateLink; strong AWS integration.
Structured Output & Tool Use

OpenAI's structured output mode and Anthropic's tool-use API are stable enough to build production pipelines around. Both use JSON Schema for type safety and eliminate the brittle regex-parsing antipattern.
Trial: Boundary-Tested, Not Yet Default

Trial technologies have proved themselves in real workloads inside the organisation or in well-documented case studies elsewhere. They carry meaningful integration risk or are still addressing stability gaps, so a new project should treat them as a deliberate choice with a defined success metric — not a default.
Orchestration Frameworks
LangChain (≥ 0.2) — mature chain and agent abstractions; large community; API surface stabilised after 0.1 breaking-change cycle.
LlamaIndex — purpose-built for RAG; strong data connector ecosystem; query pipelines provide deterministic routing.
LangGraph — graph-based multi-step agent execution; suited for workflows needing cycles and human-in-the-loop checkpoints.
Vector Stores
pgvector (PostgreSQL extension) — no new infrastructure for Postgres shops; ANN via IVFFlat/HNSW; filterable metadata; transactional consistency.
Pinecone — fully managed, serverless tier available; strong DX; proprietary hosting adds egress cost.
Weaviate — open-source with hybrid BM25+vector search; GraphQL API; object-level RBAC.
Qdrant — Rust-based, high throughput; native quantisation; payload indexing for complex filters.
Trial Pilot Template
When running a Trial, define success before starting. A one-page pilot spec prevents indefinite evaluation:

pilot:
  technology: "LangGraph"
  duration_weeks: 6
  team_size: 2

success_criteria:
  latency_p99_ms: 2000
  error_rate_pct: "<1"
  developer_satisfaction: ">=4/5"
  rollback_plan: "rewrite critical path as sequential chain"

exit:
  adopt:  all criteria met
  extend: one criterion missed by <20%
  abandon: any criterion missed by >20% or security gap found
Assess: Promising but Premature for Production

Assess items are worth an engineer's attention but not a production commitment. An Assess evaluation is time-boxed (typically two weeks), runs on synthetic or anonymised data, and produces a written technical note rather than production code.
Multi-Agent Frameworks
AutoGen (Microsoft) — async multi-agent conversation; rapid prototyping strength; production observability gaps.
CrewAI — role-based agent crews; impressive demos; hallucination guard still immature.
Semantic Kernel — .NET and Python; strong Azure integration; plugin model still stabilising.
Prompt Optimisation
DSPy — compiles natural-language modules into optimised prompt programs; research-grade API; breaking changes frequent.
Mirascope — structured prompt engineering for Pydantic-first teams; nascent ecosystem.
Observability & Guardrails
Guardrails AI — declarative output validation with RAIL specs; useful concept; performance overhead varies.
Haystack (deepset) — strong document-oriented pipelines; evaluate if your workload is pure document QA.
Assess Spike Checklist

## Assess Spike Checklist — {{ technology }}

### Setup
- [ ] Install in isolated environment (no prod credentials)
- [ ] Pin exact version (no `*` or `latest`)

### Functional gate
- [ ] Hello-world working in < 2h
- [ ] Target use case works end-to-end on synthetic data
- [ ] Document any API surface that is marked experimental

### Non-functional gate
- [ ] Latency acceptable for target p99 SLA
- [ ] Memory footprint within budget
- [ ] No credentials stored in logs or stack traces

### Risk gate
- [ ] CVE scan clean for transitive deps
- [ ] License compatible with enterprise policy
- [ ] Vendor support/roadmap documented

### Output
- [ ] Written technical note (1–2 pages)
- [ ] Radar entry updated (Assess / promote to Trial / mark Hold)
Hold: Technologies to Freeze or Migrate

A Hold entry is not a condemnation — it is a specific recommendation: stop starting new projects on this technology and, where cost-justified, migrate existing uses. Hold items in the GenAI space typically fall into three categories:
Superseded models: GPT-3.5-turbo is outperformed on quality, cost, and latency by GPT-4o-mini. New projects should not choose it.
Bare FAISS deployments: FAISS as a standalone server (not embedded) requires manual sharding, replication, and monitoring. Purpose-built vector stores provide these primitives out of the box.
LangChain ≤ 0.1: The 0.1 API surface is incompatible with current LangChain (0.2+) and LangGraph. Projects pinned to 0.1 should schedule migration.

Fine-tune-only strategies: Fine-tuning a base model without RAG was a first-wave pattern. For most enterprise knowledge tasks, RAG + prompt engineering outperforms fine-tuning at lower cost and iteration speed. Reserve fine-tuning for style/tone specialisation.
Bare PromptTemplate wrappers: Thin wrappers around raw string interpolation lack versioning, safety scanning, and observability. Promote to a proper chain or pipeline with tracing.
Quadrant Mapping for GenAI Components
Beyond the ring, the standard Thoughtworks radar divides the circle into four quadrants. For GenAI the natural split is:
Quadrant
Covers
Example entries
Platforms & Cloud
Model APIs, inference services, cloud AI platforms
Azure OpenAI, Bedrock, Vertex AI, Replicate
Frameworks & Libraries
Orchestration, RAG, evaluation, guardrails
LangChain, LlamaIndex, LangGraph, RAGAS, Guardrails AI
Data & Storage
Vector stores, document stores, embedding pipelines
pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus
Tooling & Practice
Observability, deployment, prompt versioning, eval
LangSmith, Langfuse, PromptLayer, MLflow, RAGAS
Scoring a GenAI Technology for Ring Placement
Subjective ring placement leads to political fights. A lightweight scoring rubric keeps decisions auditable:

The scoring model is a heuristic, not a rule. A single security gap (security_compliance=0) should immediately cap a technology at Assess regardless of total score, as enterprise policy likely prohibits production use without compliance evidence.
Moving Technologies Between Rings
Ring placement is not permanent. The Assess-to-Trial-to-Adopt trajectory is the expected path for a healthy ecosystem. Equally, Adopt-to-Hold happens when a better alternative reaches maturity.
Transition
Trigger
Process
Assess → Trial
Internal spike shows promising p50 latency and no blocking security issues
Pilot spec approved; pilot team funded for 4–6 weeks
Trial → Adopt
Pilot meets all success criteria; security review complete
Update radar; deprecate alternative if applicable
Adopt → Hold
Successor reaches Adopt; or CVE with no patch; or API deprecated by vendor
Freeze new projects; open migration backlog
Hold → Assess
Major new version resets risk profile (rare)
New spike — treat as if first evaluation
Radar Governance Cadence
A radar that is never updated becomes a historical document rather than a decision tool. The minimum viable process for a GenAI radar:
Quarterly blip review: each quadrant owner reviews new entrants, updates ring placements, and publishes a diff. 90 minutes, cross-functional group.
Triggered updates: a critical CVE, a vendor end-of-life announcement, or a competitor adopting a Hold item are each grounds for an out-of-band ring change.
New project gate: any new project proposing an Assess or Hold technology requires a written exception approved by the architecture review board.
Annual full refresh: re-score every Adopt and Trial entry from scratch — production evidence from two years ago may not reflect current alternatives.
Summary
Adopt contains proven, enterprise-grade foundation model APIs and managed services; use them as defaults.
Trial contains orchestration frameworks and vector stores with real-world evidence but ongoing stabilisation; run a time-boxed pilot with defined success criteria before committing.
Assess covers multi-agent frameworks and prompt optimisation tools that show conceptual promise but lack production track records; evaluate in isolation.
Hold marks superseded models (GPT-3.5), bare FAISS deployments, and first-wave fine-tune-only strategies; freeze new starts and plan migrations.
A six-dimension scoring rubric (production evidence, API stability, security, community, vendor risk, performance) makes ring placement defensible and auditable.
Quarterly review cadences and a new-project gate prevent the radar from becoming stale.
